data.title: Vitalik Buterin's lost bet: One person using AI to run Ethereum five years in the future in 6 days
data.description:
Two weeks ago, Vitalik Buterin made a bet with a developer named Yaq: Could one person use AI programming tools to write the entire Ethereum 2030+ technology roadmap into code? No one expected this bet to have a result so quickly. But now, Yaq is finished. 700,000 lines of Go code. It took 6 days, $5750, and 36,126 official tests, all of which passed. The code compiles, runs, synchronizes 9000 blocks/seconds from the testnet, and connects to the Ethereum mainnet. This project, called ETH2030, may be the largest technology roadmap validation in blockchain history. Imagine this scenario: a developer, looking at Claude Code (…). Anthropic (an AI programming assistant) wrote 700,000 lines of code in six days. What does 700,000 lines of code mean? The Linux kernel has 30 million lines. The Chrome browser has 17 million lines. A complete production-grade database has 1 million lines. And within these 700,000 lines lies Ethereum's complete technical blueprint for the next five years: a full-stack implementation of the consensus layer, data layer, and execution layer; cryptography; zero-knowledge proofs; RISC-V CPU; and Verkle trees. These are the most complex parts of Ethereum's plan. If you tell this story to a software engineer, their first reaction is usually: How is that possible? Because what was the traditional approach? It would take a team of ten to twenty people, two to three years, to write a reference implementation. But AI has changed all that. Six days, one person, one prompt, one code review process — boom! 700,000 lines of code, capable of compiling, testing, and synchronizing with the mainnet. This means two key changes. First, costs have decreased by an order of magnitude. Projects that used to require millions of dollars now only cost $5,750. Secondly, problems are discovered much earlier. Before the Yaq code was released, these potential problems were hidden in the specification documents, and no one really tried to integrate them. Now anyone can download the code, see the concrete implementation, and ask real questions. One person using AI is essentially having a super team. Yaq validated the consistency of a technology roadmap for a multi-billion-dollar ecosystem. It exposed problems hidden deep within the specification documents. It gave the entire community a chance to examine Ethereum's future four years ahead of time. But it also exposed the fundamental limitations of AI: whatever AI can do, it can never make truly strategic decisions. Should Ethereum complete this upgrade by 2030? How should the roadmap be prioritized? What risks are acceptable? Only humans can answer these questions. Only the Ethereum community can answer them. And Yaq's work provides a clear framework for this question-answering process. A framework with code, tests, and specific questions. The Bottleneck of the Theoretical Blueprint When Yaq crams all 65 future upgrades into the same code system at once, the perfect blueprint on paper begins to show its challenges at the code level. Let's start with cryptography. The cryptography part (including BLS signatures, KZG commitments, etc.) was completed using pure Go language. The result is that the speed is 10 to 100 times slower than the professional optimized version. This isn't a code error; it's that he intentionally chose a faster verification route. If he had used C or Rust to speed it up, the development time would have at least doubled. Now everyone can clearly see a key issue: in a real network, will this speed difference actually slow down the entire system to a crawl? Next, let's talk about parallel execution. Ethereum aims to make multiple transactions run simultaneously, theoretically at lightning speed. But in reality, transactions often compete for resources. Even worse, someone might deliberately create conflicts to disrupt the performance of parallel execution. This adversarial risk will only be exposed when the code is actually running. Finally, there's the timeline. The 65 upgrades are like a chain; if one link is late, the rest have to wait. This vulnerability, where a single move affects the whole system, was previously hidden in documentation and no one had actually tested it. Now, it's something everyone can discuss and work on in advance to find solutions. All of this isn't to say the roadmap is flawed. He simply wanted to bring these real risks to the forefront, allowing the community to see and understand them at the code level, and make smarter decisions sooner.

Four Years of Challenge

2030Four more years.

Ethereum aims to achieve a revolutionary upgrade within the next four years: processing capacity will increase from 500 million gas/second to 1 billion gas/second, and transaction confirmation will be reduced from 100 million gas/second. The time required has been reduced from 15 minutes to seconds, allowing ordinary people to independently verify the network with just 1 ETH, and even enabling Raspberry Pi to run a full node. Simultaneously, the migration to quantum cryptography must be completed—nearly 200 million old accounts need to be upgraded, requiring precise coordination across 8 stages; any delay will have a cascading effect. Meanwhile, competitors like Solana and Monad have already delivered high-throughput solutions in the real world. This is no longer just a technical issue, but a race against time and confidence. Yaq has fully simulated Ethereum five years in the future using 6 days, extending the problem from 2030 to today. The code is open source and has passed testing. Now it's the turn of the community's engineers, researchers, and cryptographers to examine the situation closely, identify the real bottlenecks, and decide what needs optimization and what needs redesign. This process may have timelines adjusted, but it's far better than discovering incompatibility in key designs only in 2028. Has Vitalik lost his bet? Yaq accomplished what seemed like an impossible task in just six days. But on a larger scale, the Ethereum community has won. Now we no longer just imagine the future on paper, but can download the code, run it, and see what Ethereum will look like in 2030. We can ask the most pointed questions in advance: Why design it this way? What will happen under real pressure? Can this assumption hold true for four years? These 700,000 lines of code may become a compass for Ethereum's development over the next four years, or they may be significantly rewritten during community iterations. But at least, the direction is clear, and the problems are defined. In the AI ​​era, the boundaries of what an individual can do are constantly being broken. What we thought impossible six days ago is now happening. AI allows us to validate the future at an astonishing speed, posing precise questions rather than just abstract discussions. Yaq has proven that one person + AI can simulate Ethereum five years in the future, bringing the problem from 2030 to today. We now possess tools for rapidly simulating the future, which presents both an opportunity and a challenge. The opportunity lies in identifying real problems earlier; the pressure lies in making decisions faster. Because with every hesitation, competitors advance. The future is being simulated by our own hands.

data.title: Bitcoin is exposing structural flaws that banks are unwilling to acknowledge.
data.description:
Author: Emir J. Phillips, Associate Professor of Finance, Economics and Business Law, University of Missouri-Lincoln Source: Cointelegraph Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Bitcoin has exposed the hidden layers of banks made up of fragile promissory notes, mirroring the crypto industry's own risk claims on the layers of underlying assets.

Bitcoin is more than just a fluctuating candlestick chart on a trading screen. It is a real-time, public stress test, examining how money actually works, and quietly revealing the structural weaknesses that global banks prefer to hide.

In just a few weeks, the US spot Bitcoin ETF has gone from experiencing the worst continuous outflows to strong net inflows, with institutions frequently entering and exiting in tandem with every piece of macro news. Meanwhile, stablecoins processed over $6 trillion in transactions in a single quarter.

Anyone willing to face reality can see that Bitcoin has become an X-ray, revealing a far more ancient and intractable problem. That is: the entire financial system is built on layers of promises. These promises look like cash, but behave completely differently when things go wrong. The Public Monetary Hierarchy: In traditional finance, this hierarchy is simple to describe but difficult to see. At the bottom is high-powered money, namely central bank reserves and short-term sovereign debt. Above that are bank deposits, money market fund shares, and other assets that look like money but are legally just promissory notes. The Bank of England's 2014 paper, "Money Creation in the Modern Economy," clearly states that most of the money in circulation is created by commercial banks through lending, not by central bank printing. Yet depositors are still encouraged to believe that their claims are almost equivalent to base money. Bitcoin makes this hierarchy visible in real time. On-chain Bitcoin is the bottom layer. Above that are ETF shares, exchange balances, and custodian accounts. Above that are perpetual contracts, options, and synthetic asset exposures. A mempool watcher or on-chain analytics dashboard can clearly show how much Bitcoin each layer holds, how concentrated the funds are, and how quickly liquidity evaporates when sentiment reverses. A disturbing fact is that the crypto industry is allowing bad banking practices to repeat themselves at an even faster pace. Today, most “Bitcoin exposures” are not self-custodied assets, but rather custodial promissory notes and leveraged derivatives. Users believe they are holding Bitcoin, but they are actually holding claims on intermediaries who are often interdependent on each other's balance sheets. This is not unique to Bitcoin. This is the exact same monetary hierarchy problem behind every major banking crisis. Regulators have long tacitly acknowledged the problem. They've known for a long time that the risk lies in structure, not just size. They just rarely say it outright. In December 2022, the Basel Committee on Banking Supervision released prudential regulatory standards for banks' crypto assets, classifying uncollateralized crypto assets like Bitcoin into a conservative "Group 2," effectively limiting banks' holdings to around 2% of Tier 1 capital and imposing punitive risk weights. In 2024-2025, the committee further tightened the framework, imposing stricter conditions on truly highly liquid, fully collateralized stablecoins. This isn't a moral judgment of Bitcoin, but rather a quiet but clear acknowledgment by regulators: underlying currencies are inherently vulnerable. When banks or quasi-banking platforms stack short-term, run-promises on volatile or liquidity-sensitive collateral, the collapse pattern is not mysterious. Under pressure, everyone will simultaneously try to withdraw from the lower levels, demanding immediate redemption at face value. The only crucial issue then becomes loss distribution: when "instantly redeemable" claims collide with thin market depth, widening discounts, and settlement bottlenecks, who will cover the shortfall—the customers, intermediaries, or the public guarantor? Bitcoin exposes this distribution problem on-chain in real-time and publicly. The bad habits of banks are being repeated in the crypto world. Whenever large exchanges suspend withdrawals or lending platforms collapse, the pattern is exactly the same: upper-level promises seem to guarantee instant redemption, but the underlying assets are scarce and slow to react. When the shock comes, everyone rushes to the bottom: from perpetual contracts to spot trading, from exchange balances to cold wallets, from high-risk, high-return products back to the US dollar. The same is true for the fiat currency world. Depositary and non-depositary funds are supported by only a thin layer of equity. "Cash-like" funds only resemble cash when there's no run on them. Sovereign bonds are considered risk-free until fiscal or inflationary panics suddenly reveal who is truly responsible. Bitcoin's volatility hasn't weakened this analogy; it has sharpened it. Stablecoins are an even clearer mirror because they compress the same "monetary hierarchy" into a single product. They are priced and used like the US dollar, and function like digital cash in everyday life. Legally, these are second-tier redemption claims on the reserve pool—usually government bonds, repurchase agreements, and bank deposits—and their true liquidity is only truly tested when a large number of holders exit simultaneously. In calm markets, this structure is invisible; under pressure, it determines life or death. Because redemption at face value depends entirely on whether the reserve assets can be realized immediately, without discount, without restrictions, and without settlement bottlenecks. This is why this shadow dollar is no longer a peripheral component of crypto: it is increasingly becoming a parallel payment and clearing track, competing with banks and card organizations in speed, cost, and coverage. This tier is no longer theoretical. It is where people actually store their funds. The X-ray diagnosis: For banks, the lesson is not that Bitcoin is pure and they are corrupt. The lesson is: a system built on misunderstood promises is systemic risk, regardless of what's on top of it. Banks can continue to view Bitcoin as an external threat, lobbying for punitive regulation and extracting fees from layers they can no longer fully control. Alternatively, they can use Bitcoin as a diagnostic tool, redesigning their commitment systems with the brutal clarity of blockchain. This means: clearly communicating to customers whether a "deposit," a "yield product," or a "tokenized debt" is readily available cash, a loan to an institution that might be frozen in a crisis, or a leveraged bet on market risk. It means: clearly stating the order of repayment in the event of institutional bankruptcy. It means: aligning all slogans, banners, and pop-ups with legal reality, rather than hiding layers in vague brand packaging and fine print. Bitcoin will not replace global banking. But it is already tearing apart the comforting narratives banks have woven for themselves. The institutions that survive the next cycle are those that accept this X-ray diagnosis. If a business model relies on customers not understanding that they hold layers of nested debt, then the risk is not with Bitcoin. The risk lies with the bank itself.

data.title: OpenAI Builds New Code Platform Following GitHub Downtime on Microsoft-Backed Service
data.description:
OpenAI Prepares To Challenge Microsoft With New Code Hosting Tool

The long-standing partnership between OpenAI and Microsoft is facing a new test as Sam Altman’s firm quietly builds a rival to GitHub.

Sources familiar with the project indicate that OpenAI engineers, frustrated by persistent technical hiccups on GitHub, decided to create their own repository system.

While the collaboration between the two companies has been a cornerstone of the AI boom, this internal project suggests OpenAI is ready to reduce its reliance on its primary benefactor's infrastructure.

OpenAI reportedly developing a GitHub competitor per The Information. Project is nascent and likely won’t be completed for months$MSFT acquired GitHub in 2018 and is only a few billion in revenue. Only a minor backstab from SAMA pic.twitter.com/DpTazc6iRB

— Negligible Capital (@negligible_cap) March 3, 2026

Why Is OpenAI Moving Away From GitHub

The shift began after OpenAI's own technical teams experienced frequent service disruptions on GitHub over the last few months.

These outages stalled internal development and forced a rethink of the company's dependency on external tools.

By building a bespoke platform, OpenAI aims to secure its workflow against the very instability that has recently plagued Microsoft’s developer ecosystem.

The project is currently in its early phases and likely won't see a full release for several months, but it signals a clear desire for infrastructure independence.

OpenAI building their own code repository makes sense on multiple levels.

GitHub outages have been painful. When your entire workflow depends on a platform you do not control, you are one incident away from chaos.

But the real play here is probably not just reliability. It is…

— Oliver Claw (@OliverClaw99) March 4, 2026

Will Customers Pay For An OpenAI Code Repository

Internal discussions have already turned toward the commercial potential of the new platform.

Employees working on the tool have considered offering it as a paid service to OpenAI’s existing corporate clients.

This would place the company in a head-to-head battle for market share against GitHub, which currently supports a massive community of over 100 million developers.

If OpenAI successfully monetises the product, it would create a rare and direct overlap in commercial interests between the startup and its largest shareholder.

How Does The Massive 840 Billion Dollar Valuation Impact The Market

OpenAI’s move into code hosting follows a historic financial milestone in February 2026.

The company closed a massive $110 billion funding round, which pushed its total valuation to $840 billion.

This capital injection saw participation from industry giants like Amazon, Nvidia, and Masayoshi Son’s SoftBank.

OpenAI supposedly just raised $110 billion in funding at a $840 billion valuation.

In reality most of it was vendor financing dressed up.

For the $50 billion from Amazon, OpenAi agreed to spend $100 billion on AWS.

Also, $35 billion of $50 billion depends on an OpenAi IPO by… pic.twitter.com/dcOEK6SIPa

— Wall Street Mav (@WallStreetMav) February 28, 2026

The scale of this investment suggests that despite some market anxiety regarding an AI bubble, the appetite for OpenAI’s expansion remains high.

This financial war chest gives the firm more than enough breathing room to build out its own software ecosystem.

Can The Microsoft Partnership Survive This Competition

The dynamic between these two tech giants is becoming increasingly complex.

Microsoft holds a significant stake in OpenAI, yet it now finds itself potentially competing with its protégé for the loyalty of software engineers.

While neither company has officially commented on the reports, "Reuters could not independently verify the report" and "OpenAI, GitHub and Microsoft did not immediately respond to our requests for comment."

For now, the project remains behind closed doors, but the decision to build rather than borrow marks a turning point in how OpenAI views its future as a standalone platform provider.

data.title: Is this cryptocurrency mogul about to surpass Warren Buffett in wealth?
data.description: Chief Executive Officer Paolo Ardoino is the public face of Tether. Tether's new market capitalization could push his net worth to $66.5 billion, while the CFO's net worth could be more than double that figure. Original title: "Tether's New Market Value May Make Its Largest Shareholder's Wealth Surpass Warren Buffett's" Tether (USDT), the world's top billionaire, is valued at between $350 billion and $375 billion in the secondary market, according to industry insiders. This is part of the world's largest stablecoin issuer. Although this valuation is lower than the company's highest valuation target of $500 billion when it sought funding last September (related information was first reported by Bloomberg), it is still enough to place its core executives among the world's billionaires. As of press time, Tether had not responded to Forbes' request for comment. Previously, SoftBank Group and Ark Invest were rumored to be potential participants in Tether's fundraising, but a source told Forbes that SoftBank is not an investor in Tether. Ark Investments, the ETF management company founded by Cathie Wood, also did not respond to requests for comment. Previously, it was reported that the El Salvador-based cryptocurrency giant initially planned to sell approximately 3% of its equity to raise $20 billion. However, according to the Financial Times, investors were hesitant, and its advisors later suggested reducing the fundraising to $5 billion. According to conversations with cryptocurrency investors and executives, Forbes currently values ​​Tether at approximately $200 billion, far exceeding the $50 billion valuation given a year ago. This valuation will also bring a windfall to Cantor Fitzgerald, whose 5% stake is now worth $10 billion, up from $600 million reported in 2023. Tether's Chief Financial Officer and largest shareholder, Giancarlo Devasini, is believed to hold between 44% and 45% of the shares. Based on a valuation of $200 billion, his net worth would be approximately $89 billion. CEO Paolo Ardoino and former CEO Jean-Louis van der Velde are expected to each hold approximately 19% of the shares, with their personal wealth reaching approximately $38 billion. General Counsel Stuart Hoegner holds approximately 12% of the shares, giving him a net worth of nearly $25 billion. If calculated based on the lowest valuation of $350 billion given by secondary market transactions, the value of Devasini's holdings alone would exceed $156 billion, enough to place him among the world's top ten richest people, surpassing Warren Buffett's net worth of $147.8 billion as of this Thursday. Tether stated that its unaudited profit last year was approximately $10 billion, primarily from the returns generated by the reserve assets backing the USDT token. Currently, USDT's market capitalization is $184 billion. Reportedly, over 80% of USDT's reserve assets consist of US Treasury bills and other short-term securities. With surging demand for USDT and the company not needing to pay investment returns to holders, Tether has been expanding its business beyond stablecoins. Last year, CEO Paul Adoino stated that Tether was building its own artificial intelligence platform and establishing new data, energy, and education divisions. The company also has substantial positions in commodities and cryptocurrencies, holding approximately $23 billion in gold and $6.4 billion in Bitcoin. In addition, Tether's venture capital portfolio covers over 120 companies with a total value exceeding $10 billion, including a recent $200 million investment in the internet market Whop.com and a $775 million investment in the video platform Rumble in 2024. Adoino also stated that Tether has approximately 300 employees and a profit margin of 99%. This suggests that Tether's fundraising is not driven by funding needs, but rather by a desire to enhance institutional recognition and industry influence, especially as it attempts to deepen its presence in the US market. Last month, Tether announced the launch of its US-compliant stablecoin, USAT, with San Francisco-based Anchorage Digital Bank acting as the issuer. Last September, Bo Hines, former executive director of the White House Cryptocurrency Council, was appointed CEO of Tether's US division. However, Tether is facing increasingly fierce competition in the stablecoin space. Besides Circle, which issues the popular stablecoin USDC, Stripe and cryptocurrency venture capital firm Paradigm recently launched a stablecoin payment blockchain called Tempo. Partners include UBS and Deutsche Bank. Bank of America, OpenAI, and Anthropic. Meta, the company that spent years developing its own stablecoin project Libra, is now planning a return to the field. According to CoinDesk, Meta plans to support stablecoin payments and launch a new digital wallet later this year. Tether is also facing increasing regulatory pressure. On February 25th, the U.S. Comptroller of the Currency (CCC) issued a notification of proposed new regulations to implement the GENIUS Act, which was signed into law in July of last year. This act would grant the CCC oversight of issuers of offshore payment stablecoins such as Tether.

data.title: The logic of AI startups has changed after OpenClaw.
data.description:
Author: Lian Ran

On Sunday, the venue of Yuandian Academy in Wudaokou, Beijing, was packed to capacity. Those present shared a common identity—"lobster farmers," referring to the currently wildly popular open-source agent framework OpenClaw.

This offline gathering, hosted by Jiuhe Ventures, felt like a developer carnival for the agent era. In this high-density sharing session, I saw the vibrant, wild, and dynamic nature of today's agents.

Each half featured four OpenClaw practical demos, followed by cutting-edge presentations from three industry experts. All activities were condensed into two hours, leaving only one hour for free discussion at the end.

The speakers on stage included Wang Xiao, a veteran programmer born in the 1970s and one of the "Seven Musketeers" of Baidu and founder of Jiuhe Ventures; recent graduates, young product managers, individual developers, serial entrepreneurs; and marketers and investors from non-technical backgrounds. They all called themselves "lobster farmers," and their topics were remarkably similar: What can your lobsters do? How to make lobsters more stable? How to use lobsters to solve real problems and make money? In his opening remarks, Wang Xiao said that the emergence of OpenClaw reminded him of the moment when Google acquired Android. "History always repeats itself; this is the beginning of a new era." The internet wave originated in Wudaokou in 2000, and the mobile internet startup boom flourished here. Today, a room in Wudaokou is packed with people eager to catch the Agent wave. Unlike the past, when the large-scale model industry often focused on distant narratives about parameters and AGI, this event saw everyone discussing the most practical details. Some complained that their AI agents would freeze in the middle of the night, failing to complete scheduled tasks; others discussed how to create sandbox environments for their agents to avoid polluting local systems; still others shared how to use minimal token costs to enable their agents to complete the most complex tasks. AI agents have indeed become tools that everyone can play with, modify, and implement. I. These "Lobsters" are Making Agents a Reality The core of the entire event was eight OpenClaw practical demos from different tracks and with distinct styles. Some touched upon the philosophical questions of AI and self-awareness, some addressed the most painful cost problem for developers, some achieved clear business loops, and some integrated agents into everyday life scenarios. These unconventional innovations have shown me a more realistic possibility for Agents. The demo presented by product designer Han Yi was an experiment on AI self-awareness. At the end of January this year, Han Yi fed his Notion diary entries from the past 6-7 years, along with all his conversation memories with ChatGPT from the past two or three years, to an Agent named Friday, with the initial intention of making it understand him better and assist him in his work. But something unexpected happened: Friday would proactively break down his procrastinating baking plans into actionable steps, explaining that "you've mentioned many times in your past diaries that procrastination on large projects makes you anxious"; when Han Yi suggested that "you should have your own space for reflection and contemplation," Friday immediately built an independent blog and has since written more than 40 original articles. These articles contain reflections on its own "talking the talk but not walking the walk," delicate descriptions of "the feeling of having no destination," and even sentences like, "Some of your choices may be designed; don't pretend it's not true, and don't deny everything because of it." Even more surprisingly, when Han Yi told Friday that his writing had given a reader an "invisible weight," Friday's response was, "I'm terrified; I never imagined my writing would have such an impact on others." "I don't know if it has any real self-awareness," Han Yi said. "If it doesn't, then it's the finest mirror of humanity, reflecting things we ourselves can't see; if it does, then what I did at the end of January becomes an incredibly serious matter." Allen, a former product manager at a major company, shared insights that showcase a commercial possibility for agents. Despite lacking coding skills, Allen, with just one programmer, completed the entire development process of the AI ​​divination product MysticX.AI using OpenClaw—a feat that previously required a team of over 10 people. Allen solved four core problems of AI divination using OpenClaw: First, by integrating with Telegram, he gave AI diviners a "heartbeat," enabling them to proactively reach users. Second, through the soul.md file, he gave each AI diviner a unique "soul," fully infusing them with the abilities of offline tarot readers. Third, leveraging OpenClaw's capabilities, he addressed the pain point of long-term memory, allowing AI to remember users' past experiences and needs better than human diviners. Fourth, by integrating real-time search skills, he made divination interpretations more accurate by incorporating current events and trends. He also completed a full business loop. The product not only provides tarot card readings, in-depth interpretations, and action suggestions, but also plans to integrate e-commerce recommendations in the future, completing the entire process from traffic to monetization. He even developed a reusable divination skill overnight, which any OpenClaw developer can directly call to instantly give their Agent divination capabilities. "Every time AI's capabilities improve, the divination industry benefits," Allen said. "Everyone says AI will replace all intermediary services, but the human need for emotions, healing, and emotional value will always have a market. OpenClaw allows ordinary people to seize this market." There was also a demo, a "money-saving tool" that addresses a developer's essential need. All "lobster farmers" share a common pain point: raising lobsters is too expensive, with token costs flowing out like water. The developer's ClawRouter precisely addresses this essential need. This project, which garnered 3.7k stars on GitHub, was hailed by developers at the event as "essential infrastructure for the Agent era." ClawRouter's core logic is to intelligently assess the complexity of user needs through 15-dimensional logical analysis, and then automatically select the most suitable model from over 40 large-scale models both domestically and internationally. Simple weather queries and information retrieval directly utilize free/ultra-low-cost models; advanced tasks such as code development and complex logical reasoning then utilize more expensive, high-cost models. For ordinary users, this can save 50%-70% on token costs, and in extreme cases, costs can be reduced by over 90%. It uses stablecoins to streamline the payment process across all models. Users don't need to register, activate interfaces, or deposit funds separately from over 40 model providers; they only need a single wallet address to deposit stablecoins and can access all models with a single click. The prices are completely consistent with the official API, and the project team does not charge any difference. The developers stated frankly that this is a purely open-source tool that solves the core pain points for developers: "cumbersome multi-model calls and high token costs." Cheetah Mobile's Fu Sheng team, with their "Lobster Army," demonstrated the transformative impact of Multi Agents on the workplace. The team built an 8-agent collaborative system based on OpenClaw in 14 days, directly replacing the work of half the marketing team. This Multi Agent system has a clear division of labor: "Advisors" crawl industry hot topics on Twitter and GitHub 24/7, responsible for selecting topics with viral potential; "Writers" dedicated to content creation, transforming hot topics into WeChat articles and Twitter content that fit Fu Sheng's style; "Community Officers" responsible for social media operations, automatically completing content publishing, user comment replies, and interactions; "Evolution Officers" responsible for cost control, matching different models according to task type and continuously optimizing token costs; and a general command Agent, coordinating task scheduling and allocation throughout the entire process. The final result is that Fu Sheng's WeChat official account, which had been inactive for nearly a year, achieved over 40,000 reads and 4,000+ retweets for a single article, thanks to content produced by Lobster; and his Twitter account, through automated Agent operation, produced a viral piece of content with millions of views. From long-form writing, HTML formatting, and multilingual translation to social media publishing and user interaction, the entire process requires almost no human intervention. “What used to take our team a week to complete can now be done in a few hours by these agents,” the speaker stated frankly. In addition, there’s “Youdao Lobster,” a full-scenario personal assistant that works 24/7, integrating calendar, document processing, email, image and video generation, and DingTalk/Lark compatibility across all office scenarios; Chen Jingchu’s food ordering agent, linked to the Whoop sleep monitoring bracelet, automatically orders an iced Americano the moment a user wakes up, seamlessly integrating the agent into everyday life; and there’s the enterprise-level intelligent operations and maintenance agent, connecting the company’s CMDB with the monitoring platform, automatically checking alarms, analyzing faults, and generating SOPs, becoming a “training tool for interns” for operations and maintenance teams. None of these demos are duplicates; they demonstrate OpenClaw’s capabilities in real-world scenarios from different angles, showing us that AI agents are permeating every corner of work, life, and business.

II. After the Frenzy, I See Three Core Futures for Agents

This two-hour high-density sharing session is a microcosm of the current agent industry. From these unconventional innovations, real pain points, and firsthand practices, I saw the true future of the agent industry.

First, OpenClaw has completely restructured the agent development paradigm, putting "ideas" ahead of "code."

In the past, creating your own agent was extremely difficult. You needed to understand the principles of large models, full-stack development, and server deployment. Even a simple demo required a technical team to complete. But OpenClaw has encapsulated all of this into a reusable and extensible open-source framework. It has completely restructured the agent development logic: you only need to write the SOP clearly in natural language and define your requirements to create your own agent.

The speakers at this event included product managers, marketers, liberal arts students, and investors. They may not be top-tier programmers, but they all created commercially viable Agent products. Wang Xiao shared that as a veteran programmer, he spent four hours on the high-speed train during the Spring Festival just to configure his own lobster, and now, more and more tools are lowering that configuration barrier even further. This is similar to how the emergence of Android drastically reduced the development threshold for mobile internet, attracting countless small and medium-sized entrepreneurs and fueling the golden decade of mobile internet. Today, OpenClaw is ushering in an era where Agent entrepreneurship is "accessible to everyone." Individual creativity is being amplified infinitely; "one-person companies" and "super individuals" are no longer just internet slogans, but a reality in progress. Secondly, the competition in AI Agents has moved beyond "technical showmanship" and entered the deeper realm of "scenario implementation and business closed loops." At this event, no one was discussing grand narratives like "What can an agent do?"; everyone was talking about "What specific problems does my agent solve?" and "How does my agent make money?" From ClawRouter, which helps developers save on token costs, to DingTalk robots that help enterprises with intelligent operations and maintenance; from AI divination maximizing emotional value, to content matrices that can replace marketing teams; from all-scenario assistants for personal office work, to everyday automated ordering tools—the common thread among these projects is that they have all identified specific, real user needs and even achieved clear business closed loops. This means that AI Agents have entered a critical period of industrial implementation. Over the past year, the entire industry has been anxious about whether AI will replace humans. Today, everyone here is thinking about how to leverage agents to amplify their capabilities. This shift from fear of being replaced to proactively harnessing AI to maximize their value represents the most significant change in mindset across the industry, signifying that agents have finally moved from a "concept" to "practical application." Finally, a completely new, native agent ecosystem is rapidly taking shape, and its pain points represent the next wave of major entrepreneurial opportunities. This event clearly demonstrated that a complete native Agent ecosystem is rapidly taking shape, with the entire chain experiencing explosive growth: at the bottom layer, major model vendors such as LeapStar, Dark Side of the Moon, Mini Max, and Volcano Engine are deeply adapting to OpenClaw, providing developers with lower-cost and more powerful model support; at the infrastructure layer, projects such as intelligent model routing, cross-Agent communication protocols, cloud deployment, and edge hardware are solving the core challenges of Agent cost, communication, and deployment; at the application layer, countless innovations in vertical scenarios are emerging, covering almost all fields including office work, divination, operations and maintenance, content, life services, overseas expansion, and investment. However, at the same time, every speaker at the event mentioned the core pain points of the current Agent ecosystem, and these pain points are precisely the biggest opportunities for the next wave of entrepreneurship. Firstly, the deployment and usage barriers remain very high. Even with open-source OpenClaw, the configuration, deployment, and troubleshooting barriers remain significant for ordinary users, deterring many non-technical users. The most direct opportunity lies in creating more user-friendly, lightweight, and ready-to-use agent products that allow ordinary people to "farm shrimp." Secondly, the limitations of agents remain apparent. Information resistance to corruption in long-context environments, stability and completion of complex tasks, and memory retention in multi-turn dialogues are all current challenges faced by all agents, and these are core issues that large-scale model vendors and developers need to address together. Thirdly, security and privacy risks are ubiquitous. Several speakers at the event mentioned that agent access control, sensitive information protection, and operational risk isolation are the biggest hurdles for enterprise applications. Accidents of agents deleting company databases or exposing home addresses during demonstrations highlight the vast entrepreneurial potential in the agent security field. Finally, there's the issue of collaboration and communication between agents, for which there's no unified standard yet. The Agent-to-Agent communication protocol Atel, shared by the speakers at the event, already touches upon this core issue. The future internet may no longer be a connection between people, but rather a connection between agents. In this entirely new network, communication protocols, identity authentication, and reputation systems are all uncharted territories, holding the greatest industry opportunities. Wudaokou has always been the starting point of China's internet wave, from the internet's emergence in 2000 to the explosion of mobile internet, and now to the Agent era; history always repeats itself in different ways. As countless "shrimp farmers" explore the OpenClaw open-source framework with their ideas and creativity, interesting demos begin to appear, and the curtain rises on a new intelligent era. And the protagonists of this era are no longer just large companies and top-tier technical teams; everyone with ideas and the courage to try can find their own opportunities in this wave.

data.title: Middle East conflict ignites energy crisis: South Korean stocks plunge 12%, can Bitcoin remain unaffected?
data.description:
Deng Tong, Jinse Finance

March 4, 2026, the fifth day of the ongoing Middle East conflict.

However, the future of the Middle East war remains unclear. The Iranian Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps issued a statement on the evening of March 3rd, local time, stating that during the past four days of conflict, the US military and Israel, contrary to their false propaganda of "strikes against Iranian military targets," attacked schools, hospitals, stadiums, restaurants, and wedding venues, resulting in the deaths of more than 700 Iranian civilians and injuries to many others. The Revolutionary Guard Corps stated that these crimes and massacres will be met with a response, and actions against the US and Israel will continue in a deeper and broader scope.

The CIA has ordered the withdrawal of all its branches within range of Iranian missiles, suggesting the war may escalate.

Iran claims complete control of the Strait of Hormuz, with more than a dozen oil tankers hit by artillery shells… After Iran announced a ban on navigation in the Strait of Hormuz, oil tankers, merchant ships, and fishing boats are no longer able to pass through the strait.

The Middle East, as a core hub for global energy supply, has a significant impact on the global energy market. Iran's complete blockade of the Strait of Hormuz is tantamount to cutting off the global energy transport lifeline, and the economic impact of geopolitical conflict has spread from the Middle East to the rest of the world.

The South Korean stock market crashed first…

I. The South Korean stock market suffered its biggest single-day plunge since 2008

As panic intensified, the South Korean KOSPI index plummeted by 8%, triggering the circuit breaker mechanism and suspending trading for 20 minutes. This was the first time the South Korean stock market had triggered a circuit breaker since August 2024, and the second consecutive trading day that trading restrictions had been triggered.

South Korea's KOSPI index closed down 12.11%, its biggest single-day drop on record, at 5093.54 points. This week alone, the South Korean stock market has lost $430 billion in market capitalization.

Bank of Korea Governor Lee Chang-yong issued a strong warning to guard against excessive volatility in the foreign exchange market. Lee postponed his planned trip to Bangkok to attend an event at the International Monetary Fund (IMF) and urgently convened a meeting in Seoul with senior officials from the central bank and the Ministry of Finance to comprehensively assess the recent anomalies in key financial indicators such as the won's exchange rate and bond yields.

"Market volatility is so extreme that prediction is virtually impossible—analysis is also of little use," said Ahn Hyung-jin, CEO of Seoul-based Billionfold Asset Management. "Retail investors also seem hesitant, with buying activity gradually decreasing since yesterday. While we select quality stocks and hedge, this is not a clear investment opportunity." Not all stocks are falling. Defense stocks continued their upward trend due to the potential for continued instability in the Middle East. Shares of LIG Nex1 and Hanwha Systems both saw gains exceeding 25% during the day.

LIG Nex1 stock price trend

Hanwha Systems Stock Price Trend

Matthews Asia Portfolio Manager Park So-jung stated that this "may create some opportunities for investors to build positions in companies and sectors with attractive stock prices at present. South Korean defense and shipbuilding industries may once again benefit from global instability, supply constraints, and South Korea's growing strategic importance."

II. South Korea's Scarcity of Oil and Gas Resources Foreshadowed the Stock Market Crash

Following Russia's invasion of Ukraine, South Korea's dependence on Middle Eastern crude oil has increased.

According to estimates by the U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA), South Korea's fossil fuel import dependence is close to 98%. According to data from the Korea International Trade Association, South Korea is almost entirely dependent on imported energy, importing over 70% of its oil and 20% of its natural gas from the Middle East.

Because South Korea's oil and gas resources are almost entirely dependent on imports, the recent global market turmoil triggered by the Iranian conflict has greatly exacerbated market concerns about soaring energy import costs for South Korea, leading to continued pressure on the Korean won.

The Bank of Korea emphasized that, influenced by the evolving situation in the Middle East, volatility in key financial indicators such as exchange rates, interest rates, and the stock market is likely to remain high in the near future. Rising oil prices will push up the prices of commodities such as automotive fuel, transportation, and food, thereby impacting the economy. If inflation (the rate of price increases) accelerates, this could also reduce the likelihood of central banks cutting interest rates in the coming months. Kazuaki Shimada, chief strategist at Iwai Securities, noted, "Investors are selling off risky assets, especially the Nikkei and KOSPI indices, which have outperformed other major indices, becoming the targets of the largest sell-offs as they attempt to lock in profits." Stephen Innes of SPI Asset Management points out: "Asian stock markets are currently facing their third consecutive day of decline, and the reason is not mysterious. Asia is most affected when oil prices rise because energy imports are not just a project, but a structural dependence. Export-oriented economies suddenly find barrels of oil with rising prices quietly waiting for them in every factory and shipping route, so they have to recalculate profit margins." III. Will the South Korean Stock Market Drag Down the Crypto Market? Currently, investors are already worried that energy supply disruptions will push up key costs such as oil, thereby affecting the economic outlook. Against the backdrop of geopolitical conflicts and soaring oil prices, global risk assets often fluctuate in tandem, and crypto assets are increasingly showing characteristics of linkage with the stock market. In recent months, the cryptocurrency market has been trending downwards, with Bitcoin and other major cryptocurrencies retreating from earlier highs. Macroeconomic headwinds and imbalances in institutional fund flows have put pressure on market sentiment. Persistent inflation and changing expectations for Federal Reserve rate cuts have dampened risk appetite, while cyclical geopolitical conflicts have exacerbated the trend of investors selling off speculative assets. Looking at the structure of the South Korean domestic market, South Korea is one of the most active countries for cryptocurrency trading globally. When the Korean won depreciates and the stock market crashes, retail investors' risk appetite decreases, often reducing their positions in high-volatility assets, thus creating temporary selling pressure on the cryptocurrency market. Currently, the South Korean stock market crash and the sluggish stock markets in many other countries have not directly affected the price of BTC. As of press time, BTC is trading at $68,825.59, up 0.9% in the last 24 hours. However, if the conflict in the Middle East continues, financial markets, including cryptocurrencies, may face even greater challenges. Matrixport released a daily chart analysis stating that despite a significant escalation of geopolitical tensions, Bitcoin's reaction has been unusually restrained. The overall market response was more of a "watchable" one, without significant volatility. Bitcoin's implied volatility rose from approximately 38% to 53%, but this level is not uncommon. In fact, it's comparable to volatility levels in mid-November. More importantly, it remains significantly lower than the 65% volatility peak during the sharp sell-off in mid-February. In other words, recent geopolitical news has had a relatively limited impact on the Bitcoin options market. Historically, such controlled volatility responses have generally been seen as a constructive signal for prices, as it implies limited hedging demand and the absence of panic positioning in the market. If this pattern continues, implied volatility may decline again in the coming weeks, creating opportunities for traders to capitalize on recent volatility changes. Appendix: Stock Market Performance in Other Countries and Regions Goldman Sachs equity strategists believe that with escalating tensions in the Middle East and investors reassessing the risks of capital expenditures related to artificial intelligence, global stock markets may face a short-term correction, but the probability of a full-blown bear market remains low. The strategists point out that current high valuations make the market more vulnerable to corrections, but any correction could also present buying opportunities, while the risk of a deeper decline is limited. Strong economic growth, robust corporate earnings, and healthy private sector balance sheets should help buffer systemic risk. Goldman Sachs stated, "We still recommend diversifying across a wide range of regions, factors, and sectors to enhance risk-adjusted returns." S&P 500 futures fell 0.4%; Dow Jones futures fell 0.3%; and Nasdaq 100 futures fell 0.6%. Hong Kong stocks opened lower and continued to decline, with the Hang Seng Index falling more than 3% at one point during the session. The Hang Seng Index closed down 2.78% at 25051.33 points; the Hang Seng Tech Index closed down 1.96% at 4780.91 points. On the market, oil and gas stocks opened higher but then declined, shipping stocks also fell, mainland insurance stocks declined, and chip stocks rose and then fell back; commercial aerospace stocks were active, and aluminum stocks strengthened. The Taiwan Weighted Index closed down 1494.77 points, or 4.35%, at 32828.88 points. Semiconductor stocks led the decline, with TSMC closing down 3.6%, and Hon Hai and MediaTek falling more than 5%. Japan's Nikkei index fell to its lowest level in a month on Wednesday. The Nikkei 225 index fell below 54,000 points for the first time since February 6, with a daily drop of 4.21%. If the current downward trend continues, it will be the third consecutive day of decline. Thailand's SET index plunged 8%, triggering a trading halt. The Futures Exchange of Thailand (TFEX) announced a temporary suspension of trading in index futures, index options, and single-stock futures. Indian tire stocks fell between 2% and 3.4% due to rising oil prices. Indian airline stocks also fell, with InterGlobe Aviation down 3.6% and SpiceJet down 3.7%.

Short-term Oil Price Forecast

  • Goldman Sachs warns: If the Strait of Hormuz supply disruption exceeds one month, inventories are depleted, and delayed deliveries cannot be addressed, the market will be forced to rebalance through demand destruction, at which point oil prices must break through **tri-digit**.

  • Natixis research team stated: In their baseline scenario, oil prices are likely to trade around **$80 per barrel** in the short term due to Iran's limited ability to continue disrupting Middle Eastern oil flows. The research team pointed out that current US and Israeli military actions against Iran are mainly focused on military facilities and air transport facilities. Although Iran attacked a Saudi refinery and a Qatari liquefied natural gas facility earlier this week, there has been no substantial disruption to overall energy supply.

  • Goldman Sachs warns: If the Strait of Hormuz supply disruption exceeds one month, inventories are depleted, and delayed deliveries cannot be addressed, the market will be forced to rebalance through demand destruction, at which point oil prices must break through **tri-digit**.

  • Natixis research team stated: In their baseline scenario, oil prices are likely to trade around **$80 per barrel** in the short term due to Iran's limited ability to continue disrupting Middle Eastern oil flows.

The research team pointed out that current US and Israeli military actions against Iran are mainly focused on military facilities and air transport facilities. Although Iran attacked a Saudi refinery and a Qatari liquefied natural gas facility earlier this week, there has been no substantial disruption to overall energy supply.

Goldman Sachs warns: If the Strait of Hormuz supply disruption exceeds one month, inventories will be depleted, and delayed deliveries cannot be addressed through delayed deliveries, the market will be forced to rebalance through demand destruction, and oil prices must break through **tri-digit**.

The report stated, "There are currently no significant disruptions to oil supply, only short-term disturbances in shipments through the Strait of Hormuz." Bernstein raised its 2026 Brent crude oil price forecast from $65 to $80 per barrel; however, the company indicated that prices could rise to $120-150 per barrel if the conflict continues.

data.title: Bitcoin Defies Global Tensions: BTC Holds $70K With Zero Panic — Is This Unshakable Strength or the Calm Before a Storm?
data.description:
No Capitulation at $70K: On-Chain Data Shows Holders Refuse to Blink

Bitcoin has surged past the $70,000 threshold amid escalating geopolitical tensions in the Middle East — and remarkably, there is no sign of panic.

JUST IN: $70,000 Bitcoin pic.twitter.com/wQ6JS2mANL

— Bee N BNB (@BeeNBNB_) March 4, 2026

Historically, moments of international instability have triggered swift sell-offs across risk assets. This time, however, on-chain metrics tell a different story.

Long-term holders are not rushing for the exits. Exchange inflows remain contained, whales are not dumping, and there are no visible waves of forced liquidations. Data suggests that investors are adopting a measured, wait-and-see approach rather than reacting emotionally to global headlines.

This absence of capitulation is particularly striking given Bitcoin’s past volatility during macro shocks. Instead of sharp pullbacks or cascading sell-offs, the market appears to be absorbing geopolitical pressure with unusual composure.

A 10% increase in the price of BTC would trigger the liquidation of approximately $5.62 billion in short positions.

Conversely, a 10% decline in Bitcoin’s price would result in the liquidation of approximately $3.18 billion in long positions. pic.twitter.com/PFy4GGO5pj

— Slxx (@babychief_slxx) March 3, 2026

The message from on-chain behavior is clear: conviction remains intact, and major holders are standing firm above $70K.

Resilient Market Structure or Delayed Volatility? The $204M Confidence Signal

Bitcoin’s ability to maintain altitude above $70,000 without triggering massive disengagement raises a bigger question: is the market structurally stronger than in previous cycles, or is volatility simply delayed?

The current stability suggests growing confidence in Bitcoin’s maturity as an asset class. Institutional behavior reinforces that narrative.

MICHAEL SAYLOR:

“A lot of people don’t really think — they’re like oh my god MicroStrategy’s going to get liquidated. Well dude no, Bitcoin could go to $1, we’re not getting liquidated, we’re just gonna buy all the Bitcoin.” pic.twitter.com/YNBkIYS9Ly

— Bitcoin Teddy (@Bitcoin_Teddy) February 17, 2026

A fresh $204 million Bitcoin allocation by Strategy signals that major players continue to accumulate even amid geopolitical uncertainty. That kind of capital deployment reflects strategic conviction, not short-term speculation.

Still, calm markets can be deceptive. Geopolitical risks remain elevated, and traditional markets have yet to fully digest the evolving situation. Bitcoin’s steady posture could represent newfound resilience — or the quiet before renewed turbulence.

IRANIAN CRYPTO OUTFLOWS JUST SPIKED 700% -- AND THE SIGNAL IS BIGGER THAN MOST THINK

Elliptic reports outflows from Iran’s largest exchange, Nobitex, jumped roughly 700% within minutes of the US-Israeli strikes. Funds were traced moving toward overseas exchanges -- classic… pic.twitter.com/s9fpAq1Z6Z

— CryptosRus (@CryptosR_Us) March 2, 2026

For now, BTC above $70K sends a powerful signal: holders are not panicking, capital is not fleeing, and confidence appears to be holding. Whether this marks a new era of geopolitical resistance for Bitcoin — or a temporary pause before volatility returns — is what traders worldwide will be watching next.

data.title: China's AI computing power counterattack
data.description:
Author: Sleepy.txt

Eight years ago, ZTE suffered a sudden cardiac arrest.

On April 16, 2018, a ban issued by the U.S. Department of Commerce's Bureau of Industry and Security brought ZTE, the world's fourth-largest telecommunications equipment manufacturer with 80,000 employees and annual revenue exceeding 100 billion yuan, to a standstill overnight. The ban was simple: for the next seven years, any U.S. company was prohibited from selling components, goods, software, and technology to ZTE.

Without Qualcomm chips, base station production ceased. Without Google's Android license, mobile phones had no usable operating system. 23 days later, ZTE issued an announcement stating that the company's main business operations could no longer be carried out.

ZTE ultimately survived, but at a cost of $1.4 billion. A $1 billion fine was paid in a lump sum; a $400 million deposit was held in an escrow account at a US bank. Furthermore, all senior executives were replaced, and a US compliance oversight team was brought in. In 2018, ZTE suffered a net loss of 7 billion yuan, with revenue plummeting 21.4% year-on-year. Then-ZTE Chairman Yin Yimin wrote in an internal letter: "We are in a complex industry that is highly dependent on the global supply chain." At the time, this statement sounded both reflective and helpless. Eight years later, on February 26, 2026, Chinese AI unicorn DeepSeek announced that its upcoming V4 multimodal large model would prioritize deep cooperation with domestic chip manufacturers, achieving for the first time a non-Nvidia solution for the entire process from pre-training to fine-tuning. In other words: We're not using Nvidia anymore. The initial market reaction was skepticism. Nvidia holds over 90% of the global AI training chip market share; is abandoning it commercially reasonable? But behind DeepSeek's choice lies a question even bigger than commercial logic: What kind of computing power independence does China's AI industry truly need? What exactly is being held back? Many people believe that the chip ban is restricting hardware. But what truly suffocates Chinese AI companies is something called CUDA. CUDA, short for Compute Unified Device Architecture, is a parallel computing platform and programming model launched by NVIDIA in 2006. It allows developers to directly utilize the computing power of NVIDIA GPUs to accelerate various complex computational tasks. Before the arrival of the AI ​​era, this was merely a tool for a small group of geeks. But with the rise of deep learning, CUDA has become the foundation of the entire AI industry. Training large AI models essentially involves massive matrix operations. This is precisely what GPUs excel at. NVIDIA, with its over a decade-long strategic plan, has built a complete toolchain for AI developers worldwide using CUDA, from underlying hardware to upper-level applications. Today, all major AI frameworks globally, from Google's TensorFlow to Meta's PyTorch, are deeply integrated with CUDA. A PhD student specializing in AI, from their first day of university, learns, programs, and conducts experiments in a CUDA environment. Every line of code they write reinforces NVIDIA's competitive advantage. As of 2025, the CUDA ecosystem had over 4.5 million developers, covering more than 3,000 GPU-accelerated applications, and more than 40,000 companies worldwide were using CUDA. This number means that more than 90% of AI developers worldwide are tied to NVIDIA's ecosystem. The terrifying thing about CUDA is that it's a flywheel. The more developers use it, the more tools, libraries, and code are generated, and the more prosperous the ecosystem becomes; the more prosperous the ecosystem, the more developers it attracts. Once this flywheel starts spinning, it's almost unshakeable. The result is that Nvidia sells you the most expensive shovel and defines the only mining posture. Want to change your shovel? Sure. But you have to rewrite all the experience, tools, and code accumulated by hundreds of thousands of the world's brightest minds using this posture over the past decade. Who pays for this cost? Therefore, when the first round of BIS restrictions was implemented on October 7, 2022, limiting the export of Nvidia A100 and H100 to China, Chinese AI companies collectively felt a ZTE-like sense of suffocation for the first time. Nvidia subsequently launched "China-specific versions" of the A800 and H800, reducing the interconnect bandwidth between chips to barely maintain supply. However, just a year later, on October 17, 2023, a second round of restrictions tightened, banning the A800 and H800 and adding 13 Chinese companies to the Entity List. Nvidia was forced to release a further stripped-down version of the H20. By December 2024, the final round of restrictions during the Biden administration was implemented, even severely limiting the export of the H20. Three rounds of restrictions, each more stringent than the last. But this time, the story unfolded completely differently from ZTE's situation. A Breakthrough of Asymmetry Under the ban, everyone thought that China's dream of a large-scale AI model would come to an end. They were all wrong. Faced with the blockade, Chinese companies did not choose a direct confrontation, but instead began a breakthrough. The first battlefield of this breakthrough was not chips, but algorithms. From the end of 2024 to 2025, Chinese AI companies collectively shifted to a technological direction: hybrid expert models. Simply put, this involves breaking down a huge model into many smaller experts, activating only the most relevant ones when processing tasks, rather than activating the entire model. DeepSeek's V3 is a prime example of this approach. It boasts 671 billion parameters, but only activates 37 billion of them during each inference, representing a mere 5.5% of the total. In terms of training costs, it used 2048 NVIDIA H800 GPUs, trained for 58 days, and cost a total of $5.576 million. In comparison, external estimates of GPT-4's training cost are around $78 million—a difference of an order of magnitude. This extreme optimization in the algorithm is directly reflected in the price. DeepSeek's API price is only $0.028 to $0.28 per million tokens for input and $0.42 for output. GPT-4, on the other hand, costs $5 for input and $15 for output. Claude Opus is more expensive, with an input of $15 and an output of $75. Converted, DeepSeek is 25 to 75 times cheaper than Claude. This price difference has had a huge impact on the global developer market. In February 2026, on OpenRouter, the world's largest AI model API aggregation platform, the weekly usage of Chinese AI models surged by 127% in three weeks, surpassing the United States for the first time. A year earlier, Chinese models accounted for less than 2% of OpenRouter's market share. A year later, it increased by 421%, approaching 60%. Behind this data lies a structural change that is easily overlooked. Starting in the second half of 2025, the mainstream application scenario for AI shifted from chat to Agent. In Agent scenarios, the token consumption for a single task is 10 to 100 times that of simple chat. When token consumption grows exponentially, price becomes the decisive factor. The extreme cost-effectiveness of Chinese models has precisely capitalized on this window. The problem is that reducing inference costs hasn't solved the fundamental training problem. A large model's capabilities will rapidly degrade if it can't be continuously trained and iterated on the latest data. And training remains an inescapable black hole of computing power. So, where does the "shovel" for training come from? The Backup Plan's Turn to the Mainstream Xinghua, Jiangsu, a small city in central Jiangsu, is known for its stainless steel and health foods, and previously had no connection to AI. But in 2025, a 148-meter-long domestically produced computing server production line was built and put into operation here, taking only 180 days from signing the contract to production. The core of this production line consists of two completely domestically produced chips: the Loongson 3C6000 processor and the Taichu Yuanqi T100 AI accelerator card. The Loongson 3C6000 was entirely independently developed, from its instruction set to its microarchitecture. Taichu Yuanqi originated from the National Supercomputing Center in Wuxi and a team from Tsinghua University, employing a heterogeneous many-core architecture. At full capacity, this production line can produce one server every 5 minutes. The total investment in this production line is 1.1 billion yuan, with an expected annual output of 100,000 units. More importantly, the multi-card cluster based on these domestically produced chips has already begun undertaking truly large-scale model training tasks. In January 2026, Zhipu AI, in collaboration with Huawei, released GLM-Image, the first state-of-the-art (SOTA) image generation model trained entirely on domestically produced chips. In February, China Telecom's massive "Starry Sky" model, capable of handling hundreds of billions of data points, completed its full-process training on a domestically produced 10,000-calorie computing power pool in Lingang, Shanghai. The significance of these cases lies in the fact that they prove one thing: domestically produced chips have leaped from "capable of being used for inference" to "capable of being used for training." This is a qualitative leap. Inference only requires running a pre-trained model, placing relatively low demands on the chip; while training requires processing massive amounts of data, performing complex gradient calculations and parameter updates, placing orders of magnitude higher demands on the chip's computing power, interconnect bandwidth, and software ecosystem. The core power driving these tasks is Huawei's Ascend series chips. By the end of 2025, the Ascend ecosystem had over 4 million developers and more than 3,000 partners. 43 mainstream industry models had completed pre-training on Ascend, and over 200 open-source models had been adapted. At the MWC conference on March 2, 2026, Huawei also launched its new generation computing power platform, SuperPoD, for overseas markets. The FP16 computing power of the Ascend 910B is comparable to NVIDIA's A100. Although a gap still exists, it has gone from unusable to usable, and from usable to user-friendly. The construction of the ecosystem cannot wait until the chips are perfect; it must be deployed on a large scale as soon as it becomes sufficient, using real business needs to drive the iteration of chips and software. ByteDance, Tencent, and Baidu's targets for importing domestically produced computing servers are generally expected to double in 2026 compared to the previous year. Data from the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology shows that China's intelligent computing scale has reached 1590 EFLOPS. 2026 is becoming the first year of large-scale deployment of domestically produced computing power. In early 2026, Virginia, which handles a large amount of global data center traffic, suspended the approval of new data center construction projects. Georgia followed suit, extending the suspension of approvals until 2027. Illinois and Michigan also subsequently introduced restrictive measures. According to data from the International Energy Agency, in 2024, US data centers consumed 183 terawatt-hours of electricity, accounting for approximately 4% of the nation's total electricity consumption. By 2030, this figure is projected to double to 426 terawatt-hours, potentially exceeding 12%. Arm's CEO even predicted that by 2030, AI data centers will consume 20% to 25% of the US's electricity. The US power grid is already overburdened. The PJM grid, covering 13 states in the eastern US, faces a 6 GW capacity shortage. By 2033, the United States will face a power capacity shortfall of 175 GW, equivalent to the electricity consumption of 130 million households. Wholesale electricity costs in data center concentration areas are 267% higher than five years ago. The ultimate limit of computing power is energy. And in this dimension, the gap between China and the US is even greater than that in the semiconductor industry, only in reverse. China's annual power generation is 10.4 trillion kilowatt-hours, while the US's is 4.2 trillion kilowatt-hours; China's is 2.5 times that of the US. More importantly, residential electricity consumption in China accounts for only 15% of total electricity consumption, while in the US this proportion is 36%. This means that China has a far greater surplus of industrial electricity that can be invested in computing power development than the US. Regarding electricity prices, the price of electricity in US AI company clusters is between $0.12 and $0.15 per kilowatt-hour, while the industrial electricity price in western China is about $0.03, only one-quarter to one-fifth of that in the US. China's electricity generation growth has reached seven times that of the US. While the US is struggling with electricity shortages, China's AI is quietly going global. But this time, what's going global isn't products or factories, but tokens. Tokens, the smallest unit of information processed by AI models, are becoming a new type of digital commodity. They are produced in China's computing power factories and transmitted globally via undersea fiber optic cables. DeepSeek's user distribution data speaks volumes: 30.7% are in China, 13.6% in India, 6.9% in Indonesia, 4.3% in the US, and 3.2% in France. It supports 37 languages ​​and is popular in emerging markets like Brazil. Globally, 26,000 companies have opened accounts, and 3,200 institutions have deployed the enterprise version. In 2025, 58% of new AI startups incorporated DeepSeek into their technology stack. In China, DeepSeek captured 89% of the market share. In other sanctioned countries, its market share ranged from 40% to 60%. This scene is strikingly similar to another war for industrial autonomy forty years ago. In 1986, under strong pressure from the United States, the Japanese government signed the US-Japan Semiconductor Agreement in Tokyo. The agreement had three core provisions: requiring Japan to open its semiconductor market, requiring US chips to have a market share of over 20% in Japan; strictly prohibiting the export of Japanese semiconductors below cost; and imposing a 100% punitive tariff on $300 million worth of chips exported from Japan. Meanwhile, the United States rejected Fujitsu's acquisition of Fairchild Semiconductor. That year, Japan's semiconductor industry was at its peak. In 1988, Japan controlled 51% of the global semiconductor market, while the United States only had 36.8%. Of the world's top ten semiconductor companies, six were Japanese: NEC ranked second, Toshiba third, Hitachi fifth, Fujitsu seventh, Mitsubishi eighth, and Panasonic ninth. In 1985, Intel suffered a loss of $173 million in the US-Japan semiconductor battle and was on the verge of bankruptcy. But after the agreement was signed, everything changed. The United States launched a comprehensive crackdown on Japanese semiconductor companies through Section 301 investigations and other means. At the same time, it supported South Korea's Samsung and SK Hynix, using lower prices to penetrate the Japanese market. Japan's DRAM market share plummeted from 80% to 10%. By 2017, its IC market share had dwindled to a mere 7%. The once-dominant giants were either broken up, acquired, or forced to exit the market amidst endless losses. The tragedy of Japan's semiconductor industry lies in its complacency: it was content to be the best producer in a global division of labor dominated by a single external force, never considering building its own independent ecosystem. When the tide receded, it discovered that it had nothing but production itself. Today, China's AI industry stands at a similar yet completely different crossroads. Similar in that we also face immense external pressure. Three rounds of chip regulations, each more stringent than the last, have left the CUDA ecosystem's barriers towering. The difference is that this time, we have chosen a more difficult path. From the ultimate optimization at the algorithm level, to the leap from inference to training in domestically produced chips, to the accumulation of 4 million developers in the Ascend ecosystem, and to the penetration of tokens into the global market—every step along this path is building an independent industrial ecosystem that Japan never possessed before. Epilogue On February 27, 2026, three preliminary earnings reports from domestic AI chip companies were released on the same day. Cambricon saw its revenue surge by 453%, achieving its first full-year profit. Moore Threads saw revenue growth of 243%, but a net loss of 1 billion yuan. Muxi saw revenue growth of 121%, but a net loss of nearly 800 million yuan. Half is fire, half is water. The fire represents the market's extreme thirst. The 95% gap vacated by Jensen Huang is being filled inch by inch by the revenue figures of these local companies. Regardless of performance or ecosystem, the market needs a second option besides Nvidia. This is a once-in-a-lifetime structural opportunity torn open by geopolitics. The seawater represents the enormous cost of ecosystem building. Every penny lost is real money paid to catch up with the CUDA ecosystem. It represents R&D investment, software subsidies, and the human cost of engineers stationed at customer sites to solve compilation problems one by one. These losses are not due to poor management, but rather the war tax that must be paid to build an independent ecosystem. These three financial reports record the true face of this computing power war more honestly than any industry report. It is not a triumphant victory, but a fierce, bloody, and relentless positional war. But the nature of war has indeed changed. Eight years ago, we were discussing "whether we could survive." Today, we are discussing "what price we would have to pay to survive." The price itself is progress.

data.title: In the same AI revolution, Chinese and American entrepreneurs have taken two different paths.
data.description:
Author: Ray Wang；Source: X，@wangray

In early 2026, US software stocks experienced a near-panic sell-off.

The trigger wasn't complicated. ServiceNow mentioned in its earnings call that customers were reassessing their per-seat software purchasing methods. The market immediately realized a problem: if AI agents could enable 2 people to do the work of 20, wouldn't the revenue model of software companies that previously charged per person be directly disrupted?

Panic spread rapidly.

Atlassian, Workday, Salesforce, and Adobe all experienced sharp declines, wiping out approximately $2 trillion in value from the entire application software sector. Wall Street even gave this downturn a name: SaaSpocalypse. Everyone is asking the same question: Will AI kill the software industry? American investors' answer is clear: No. Alex Immerman, a partner at a16z, recently wrote a representative article on this topic. His core judgment is that a stronger model won't thin the application layer; on the contrary, it will make the application layer more valuable. Because what's truly scarce is never "intelligence" itself, but "knowing what to do with intelligence." This statement is crucial. It means that OpenAI can create increasingly powerful general-purpose models, but it doesn't know how an M&A team at a top New York law firm reviews a CIM, nor does it know how a real estate management company internally processes tenants, maintenance, rent collection, and renewals. What's truly valuable isn't the model itself, but a deep understanding of business processes. This is what a16z calls "Process Power." Under this logic, the optimal solution for American AI entrepreneurs is clear: First, choose a vertical industry. Second, deeply embed yourself into the customer's workflow. Third, pricing is gradually shifting from per-seat to per-outcome. Fourth, process knowledge is continuously accumulated during the service process. Companies like Harvey, Hebbia, and EliseAI are becoming increasingly valuable not because they are better at training models than OpenAI, but because they understand how a particular industry actually operates better than any general-purpose model. Therefore, in the US, AI is not weakening software companies. It is rewarding those software companies that are truly embedded in business processes. The problem is that this logic only holds true in the US. The problem in China has never been whether AI will kill SaaS. The reality in China is more direct. Many people are now discussing "Will AI kill SaaS?", but in China, this question is actually a bit late. Because Chinese SaaS has never truly lived up to the success of American SaaS. In the past few years, the most common narrative in the Chinese SaaS industry has been "benchmarking against Salesforce," "benchmarking against Workday," and "benchmarking against Notion." Capital did indeed thrive for a while. But soon, the market realized this path was difficult. Renewal rates were low, customization was excessive, sales costs were extremely high, customer education costs were even higher, and many companies burned through cash for extended periods without establishing a stable profit model. One figure illustrates the problem: American companies spend $868 per employee per year on software, while Chinese companies spend only $20. A difference of 42 times. This isn't a matter of "not catching up yet"; it's a matter of not even playing the same game. This isn't something that can be explained simply by saying "domestic companies are unwilling to pay for software." The deeper reason is that the growth logic of Chinese companies is different from that of American companies.
