A person who previously worked as COO at a large AI company and later started a business in enterprise services gave me a great formula: **Enterprise Value = Traffic × Conversion Ability.** In China, many companies assume that conversion ability has already been pushed to its limit; what truly differentiates them is traffic. Can you get more exposure, more leads, and more orders on platforms like Douyin, Taobao, WeChat, and news feeds? When companies focus their growth on "grabbing traffic," their budgets naturally prioritize traffic acquisition. Customer acquisition costs are fixed, while backend system costs become compressible. SaaS budgets are often eaten up by frontend advertising before they can even grow. This is only the first layer. The second layer is more realistic: within Chinese companies, tools and people are often not complementary but rather in competition for budgets. If you spend 1 million to buy a SaaS solution, theoretically, that 1 million can't be spent on people. The problem is, people are usually a hard requirement within an organization, even tied to staffing levels, management authority, and departmental boundaries. Tools are more easily cut. Therefore, many SaaS projects fail not because the product is bad, but because they enter a naturally disadvantageous position. The third reason is China's labor structure over the past two decades. In a market where problems can be solved by simply adding more people, companies naturally develop the habit of: when there's a need, add people first. Processes, organization, and management methods are all designed around the idea that "people can fill the gaps." Over time, many processes are not designed for software, but for flexible manpower. This creates a closed loop: Because hiring is cheap, companies prioritize hiring. Because they keep hiring, processes are designed around people. Because processes are designed around people, standardized software is difficult to embed directly. Because a lot of customization is needed, the SaaS business model doesn't run smoothly. This is the fundamental reason why Chinese SaaS has always struggled to replicate American SaaS. Why are China and the US taking different paths in AI? Here, the truly crucial divergence occurs. In the US, companies are already accustomed to paying for software, and processes are already digitized. The most natural result of AI's introduction is a thicker application layer, allowing software to be more deeply embedded in workflows. The best path for American entrepreneurs is to develop vertical AI applications and accumulate process power. In China, many companies are unwilling to pay for software separately, but they are willing to pay for human resources long-term. Once AI can significantly replace human labor, the opportunity immediately arises. Therefore, in China, the optimal solution for AI is often not "selling a better SaaS," but rather **directly delivering a cheaper labor outcome**. This difference is crucial. Many AI startups in the US are essentially selling "thicker software." Many AI startups in China are essentially selling "cheaper people." If you look at the marketing rhetoric of many Chinese AI application companies, the focus is almost never on "our system is more advanced," but rather on "I can save you several people," "I can reduce the workload of 20 people to 2," "I can reduce your operating costs by 80% to 90%." This isn't a coincidence in marketing copy; it's determined by the market structure. The Chinese SaaS market is approximately $17.5 billion. However, the broader Chinese human resources services market—including outsourcing, staffing, and agency operations—is $480 billion, 27 times the size of SaaS. Companies are spending their money on people, not software. AI should be where the money is. Therefore, I increasingly feel that a crucial judgment is: In the US, AI's main competitor is legacy software. In China, AI's main competitor is legacy human resources. This also means that if Chinese entrepreneurs are still copying the US SaaS script, they are likely heading in the wrong direction from the start. Your customers may not want to buy a new tool. What they want to hear is: Can I help you reduce your current 20 people to 2? On the surface, they seem like two different paths, but at their core, they lead to the same place. At this point, many people will feel that the paths of China and the US are completely divergent. On the surface, that is indeed the case. The US is building a thicker application layer, while China is focusing on cheaper labor substitution. One resembles a software business, the other a service business. But if you broaden your perspective, you'll find they both ultimately lead to the same core asset: a structured understanding of business processes. Harvey's strength isn't due to its chat application or its partnership with GPT. Its true strength lies in its growing understanding of how a law firm reviews contracts, writes memos, handles approvals, and manages risks. A Chinese AI-powered operations team's increasing strength isn't due to its ability to execute automated scripts, but rather its ability to adapt to changes in platform rules, review standards, ad placement logic, and customer service scenarios, ultimately understanding the business better than the client themselves. One sells software, the other sells results. One appears more like a product, the other more like a service. But the underlying barriers are actually the same thing: **whoever understands the business better has a stronger barrier to entry.** This is what I find most interesting about this round of AI. AI appears to be lowering the barrier to entry. Code is cheaper, models are cheaper, and prototypes are easier to create. But what truly differentiates us is increasingly focusing on one thing: whether you've truly entered a particular industry and genuinely understand how it operates daily. Model capabilities will become increasingly similar. General capabilities will become increasingly commoditized. What's truly difficult to replicate will increasingly be the tacit process knowledge, gray-scale judgment, troubleshooting experience, organizational habits, and customer scenarios. The next step for Process Power is not static knowledge, but dynamic adaptation. Taking it a step further, the most valuable form of Process Power in the future may not be "I have a process knowledge base," but rather "I have a capability to continuously adapt to change." Because the real world is not static. Platform rules change, interfaces change, review standards change, user behavior changes, and organizational processes change. A set of best practices you accumulate today may need to be rewritten in six months. Therefore, a truly powerful system not only knows what to do now, but can also quickly identify, quickly fix, and quickly adapt to changes in the environment. This is the deeper moat I understand in the AI ​​era: it's not static process knowledge, but dynamic response capabilities. Every time you solve a new problem, the system accumulates another sample of real-world changes. The first time fixing a problem takes 4 hours, the fifth time it might only take 30 minutes, and the twentieth time it might be automated. When this capability begins to compound, it's no longer just "automation," but approaches a kind of self-evolution at the business level. This will become increasingly important, whether you're developing vertical AI software in the US or using AI for labor replacement in China. A conclusion for entrepreneurs: So, back to the initial question: Will AI kill the software industry? My answer is: AI isn't killing the software industry uniformly. AI is fragmenting it. In the US, the most vulnerable are applications with only a thin layer of packaging and no process barriers. They will be quickly swallowed up by the model. In the US, the companies with the greatest opportunity are those that are truly embedded in workflows and accumulate process power. The stronger the model, the stronger they usually are. In China, what will truly be broken down is not SaaS, but a large number of inefficient, repetitive, and highly standardized human positions. In China, the companies with the greatest opportunity are not "creating another Chinese version of Salesforce," but those that can directly replace human labor, deliver results directly, and accumulate process assets in the process of service. So if you're starting a business in the US today, you should be thinking: Can I enter a sufficiently deep vertical industry and understand its processes better than the customer themselves? If you were starting a business in China today, you should be thinking: Can I package AI into a productivity unit with clear results, direct ROI, and a higher return on investment than hiring people? These two paths seem different, but they ultimately lead to the same conclusion: **code will depreciate, models will iterate, only a deep understanding of the business will become increasingly valuable.** Because in the AI ​​era, the difficulty is never in intelligence itself. The difficulty lies in knowing what to do with that intelligence.

data.title: Three years ago, OpenAI predicted that professions unaffected by AI would be crushed at four times the rate of change.
data.description:
Author: Boyang

On February 27th, the American fintech company Block announced a 40% layoff, approximately 4,000 employees, as part of its full transformation into an AI company. The AI ​​concept dramatically caused its stock price to surge by over 20%. This case, from a company that is not particularly significant in Silicon Valley, reveals the potential economic ripple effects that the rapid development of AI can trigger.

The "Marking the Boat to Find the Sword" incident of 2023 and the sudden arrival of a harsh winter in 2026

Let's turn back the clock to March 2023, when ChatGPT had just ignited the world. Researchers from OpenAI, in collaboration with several universities, published a landmark paper, "GPTs are GPTs" (Generative Pre-trained Models are General Purpose Techniques).

At that time, the OpenAI team used a scoring model based on task exposure. They concluded that approximately 80% of the US workforce would be affected by at least 10% of their job tasks, while approximately 19% of workers would see more than 50% of their tasks affected.

Even more interestingly, they discovered a "high-salary paradox." Unlike the past few decades where automation technologies (such as robotic arms) were always the first to eliminate blue-collar workers, in the GPT era, the higher the salary for cognitive jobs, the higher the exposure. On the skill tree, programming and writing skills showed a strong positive correlation with AI exposure, while science and critical thinking were considered "safe zones." At that point in time, researchers clearly identified a limitation: they hadn't factored in multimodal capabilities such as vision. They hadn't even considered tool-using capabilities. Within the 2023 framework, AI remained a brain confined to a screen, only capable of processing text and code. Their upper limit prediction was that this restructuring could take up to a decade (until 2032) to fully unfold. Fast forward to early 2026, and global IT services giant Cognizant released an updated report on their 2023 research, "New Jobs, New World 2026." The report begins by stating, "What we originally predicted would take ten years (until 2032) to happen has now unfolded before our eyes six years earlier." Data shows that... Today, 93% of jobs in the US are affected by AI to varying degrees. Cognizant uses a metric called the Velocity Score, which, simply put, measures how quickly your job is being taken over by AI. As shown in the image below, the average annual growth rate of AI exposure for all professions had previously been 2%, but it has now jumped to 9%, equivalent to an acceleration of 4.5 times. This means that professions that seemed immune to AI in 2023 are now being drawn into the AI ​​race at four times the speed.

Specifically, in terms of job positions, the proportion of positions with task exposure exceeding 50% surged from 0% in 2023 to 30% (originally predicted to be only 15% by 2032), while positions with at least 25% exposure to all tasks increased by 17% to 69%. According to Cognizant's calculations, in the United States alone, this is equivalent to transferring $4.5 trillion in human labor costs to AI, approximately 15% of the US GDP. Where does this acceleration come from? The report uses a very fine classification to depict the stratification of different exposure levels. E0 (No exposure) - Completely no exposure, 32% of tasks. Requires supporting software but feasible, 17% of tasks. E3 (With image capability) - Feasible with added vision capabilities, 17% of tasks. Full automation - Fully automated, 10% of tasks (this is the biggest leap from 1% to 10% between 2023 and 2026). From this classification, we can see that the biggest changes come from E1 to E3, which is LLM plus multimodal (eyes and ears) and advanced reasoning (brain), along with the accompanying Agentic AI agents (hands and feet). ChatGPT alone has a limited impact (10%), but once agents can use specialized tools, the impact expands to 27%, and when combined with the scope of visual processing, it directly covers 44% of the work. For example, an AI cannot replace a plumber who can simply observe or think. However, when AI can "understand the location of the leak, deduce the possible causes, generate a repair plan, and automatically order parts," then his job is restructured. Although he still needs to tighten the screws, he is no longer needed for the initial diagnosis and subsequent reporting. This explosion of combined capabilities has led to several consequences unimaginable in 2023. First, management is no longer safe. Once upon a time, CEOs and executives believed that coordination, budget allocation, and decision-making were uniquely human. But by 2026, agents will be able to autonomously schedule events, reallocate budgets based on spending patterns, and track project progress. Cognizant data shows that CEOs' AI exposure has surged from 25% to over 60%.

Second,The defenses of blue-collar workers and the physical world have been breached.Construction workers, mechanics, and plumbers were once considered low-risk areas unreachable by AI.Construction workers, mechanics, and plumbers were once considered low-risk areas unreachable by AI. However, with the support of multimodal and AR wearable devices, AI can now analyze on-site photos to diagnose pipe leaks or read building blueprints. AI exposure in the construction industry has risen from 4% to 12%, and in the transportation industry, it has surged from 6% to 25%. A plumber won't lose his job, but his future work will be directly directed by AI headsets. Cognizant selected the six professions most affected by AI, ranked by the percentage of tasks that can be completed by AI. At the top of the list are finance managers, whose work can be handled by AI in 84% of its tasks. In other words, AI can take over core tasks such as financial planning, budget analysis, and risk assessment. Computer and mathematics-related positions follow closely behind, with an impact rate of 67%. The two main categories, business and financial operations, and office and administrative support, both accounted for between 60% and 68%. Legal professions accounted for 63%, and management positions (including senior management) for 60%. The changes in the software development field have been particularly noticeable in the past few months. Boris Cherny, principal engineer at Anthropic, revealed a surprising figure in January: almost 100% of their company's code was written using their own AI products, Claude Code and Opus 4.5. "Personally, I haven't written any code myself for over two months, not even minor modifications," Cherny said. "Yesterday I submitted 22 pull requests, and the day before 27, all written by Claude." Of course, they found that 34 occupations had absolutely no task exposure. These occupations were all purely manual, on-site, and handicraft work: bricklayers, butchers, dishwashers, stonemasons, tire repairmen... These changes may indicate that the polarization of the labor market will intensify. Highly skilled workers are becoming more productive with AI, while low-skilled workers are stuck in low-paying, arduous jobs that cannot be automated. The middle-skilled white-collar workers, whose jobs can be automated but not fully automated, are in the most precarious situation. This is precisely what is happening in the current job market. Big data doesn't lie: the entry point is closed, and the middle layer is collapsing. Predictions seem urgent, but what has actually happened in the labor market in the past? When we turn our attention to the big data of online job postings compiled by institutions such as Lightcast, PwC, Indeed, and Stanford over the past three years (2023-2026), we find many aspects that align with predictions. The report at the time predicted that high-paying jobs generally exhibited higher exposure, with exposure positively correlated with the required programming and writing skills, and negatively correlated with scientific and critical thinking skills. These predictions have been validated in the job posting data. Moreover, the general direction is correct: the more knowledge-intensive, text-intensive, and rule-intensive the job, the faster AI penetration; the more physically demanding, on-site judgment, and interpersonal interaction the job requires, the lower the exposure. The part that was surpassed was speed. The 2023 report predicted these changes would unfold within a decade, but significant structural changes were seen in just three years. More importantly, the report at the time emphasized that our exposure measurement did not distinguish between labor enhancement and labor substitution, implying that technological feasibility did not equate to actual adoption. However, in reality, corporate adoption has been much faster than academia anticipated. Looking deeper, we see a picture researchers have named "Hybrid Transformation." This mild academic term cannot conceal its essence: an ongoing class restructuring. First, the biggest beneficiaries of this transformation are AI users. As of the end of 2025 and the beginning of 2026, purely "AI skills positions" still accounted for a relatively small percentage of the overall recruitment market, at approximately 4.2%. However, its growth rate is extremely rapid, with the mention rate of generative AI-related positions increasing more than threefold compared to 2023. Moreover, starting from the end of 2023, recruitment began to diverge; while all recruitment decreased, recruitment specifically for AI continued to rise. The market has rewarded this very small group of people who have mastered the new tools of productivity with extremely high rewards. PwC and Lightcast's data are highly consistent: Within the same profession, positions requiring AI skills typically command a 15% to 30% salary premium, and in some core knowledge areas (such as lawyers and financial analysts), the salary difference can even widen to 56%. This is by no means a "common prosperity" for all working people, but rather a dramatic polarization of the wage structure. Companies are willing to pay high salaries to those who can use AI to increase output tenfold, while simultaneously freezing the salaries of those who only perform traditional, repetitive mental labor. Secondly, there's the "hidden death" of entry-level white-collar jobs over the past three years. AI hasn't caused a precipitous collapse in total employment at the macro level (currently, the total number of hires is still fluctuating normally after the pandemic), but in the "newbie village," a massacre has already occurred. An analysis by the Stanford Digital Economy Lab, combining ADP salary data with tens of millions of resumes, shows that since the ChatGPT outbreak at the end of 2022, employment among young people aged 22-25 in industries with high AI exposure has contracted significantly (down by about 6%, and even down by 20% in fields such as software development), while employment among older, more experienced employees in the same industries continues to grow. A causal identification study based on 285 million US job postings estimated that after the release of ChatGPT, the number of job postings for highly AI-substitutable occupations decreased by an average of about 12% compared to less substitutable occupations. Moreover, this effect was stronger for entry-level positions that did not require high education or much experience, with decreases of 18% and 20% respectively. The decrease for administrative support positions was even closer to 40%.

This is called "Seniority-Biased Technological ChangeIn the past, large companies needed to hire a large number of fresh graduates and junior employees to do basic code reviews, data cleaning, drafting financial reports, and organizing legal documents. Now, senior employees can handle these dirty and tedious tasks with the help of a few AI agents. A study covering 62 million workers found that employment in entry-level positions in companies using GenAI declined significantly starting in the first quarter of 2023. Companies aren't laying off people; they're simply not hiring. This is because mid-level employees can do more work with AI. Companies are even too lazy to fire entry-level employees, as they can let experienced staff leave naturally without hiring new ones. This gradual, insidious layoff is beyond the reach of labor laws. The "first step" for young people to enter the career ladder has been taken away by AI. The final trend is that task rewriting is replacing career extinction. In 2013, Oxford University made a famous and alarming prediction that "47% of jobs will be automated" in the future. Why hasn't this happened yet? Because a job is a shell, containing countless "tasks." Data from Indeed and Revelio Labs shows that job titles haven't disappeared, but the "job descriptions (JDs)" written by HR in job postings have been rewritten. In finance, clerical, and junior coding positions, the proportion of tasks easily replaced by AI, such as "daily reconciliation" and "generating standard code," is declining sharply. Instead, companies are requiring applicants to possess abilities in "complexity management," "AI system guidance," "edge case solving," and "quality verification and judgment." This confirms Cognizant's insight. Even if AI takes over 39% of the tasks in a job, the remaining 61% still requires humans to integrate the AI's work and place it within a larger business context. The next year or two will be a period of restructuring with "humans + AI." Pure executors will be eliminated, leaving behind judges and coordinators. However, the number of judges and coordinators will also decrease. One experienced judge plus AI can do the work of ten junior executors, meaning companies only need one-fifth of their original workforce. So-called human-machine collaboration is essentially about replacing the majority of ordinary people with a small number of elites plus AI.

Towards 2028: The Agent Singularity and the Global Intelligence Crisis

What would happen if we extended the current "structural squeeze" in the job market and the evolutionary curve of Agent technology forward?

Before answering this question, let's look at what has happened in the past three years?

In 2023, OpenAI said it would take ten years to change the job structure; in 2026, Cognizant said a huge change had already occurred. In 2023, fully automated tasks accounted for 1%; in 2026, that number was 10%. In 2023, entry-level positions were still being filled normally; in 2026, the number of job openings for entry-level positions in AI-high exposure industries had decreased by 18%-40%. If this acceleration doesn't stop, what will 2028 be like? Citrini Research, in an in-depth projection titled "The Global Intelligence Crisis of 2028: A Thought Experiment in Financial History from the Future," depicts a chilling post-singularity world. In this scenario, the timeline is set in June 2028. Between 2026 and 2027, the market is immersed in an absurd frenzy. Due to the large-scale deployment of AI agents, the S&P 500 and Nasdaq have soared, and corporate profits have repeatedly reached new highs. Labor productivity has reached its highest level since the 1950s. Agents that create products don't need sleep, don't need health insurance, and don't get sick. But economists soon discovered a fatal problem: ghost GDP. This refers to wealth that shines in national accounts but never circulates in the real economy. Why? Because a GPU cluster in North Dakota is doing the work of 10,000 white-collar workers in Manhattan, and machines don't buy coffee, pay rent, go to the movies, or go on vacation. The consumer-driven market, which accounts for 70% of the US economy, is beginning to wither. If we extend the current "structural squeeze" in the job market and the evolutionary curve of agent technology forward, this term may well become a reality. Past technological innovations (such as cloud computing and the internet) were mostly capital expenditures (CapEx), which created massive upstream and downstream jobs. However, the introduction of agents is a direct replacement for operating expenses (OpEx). By 2026, when agentic tools (such as advanced versions of Claude Code) experience a leap in capabilities, enterprise CIOs will find they can replace hundreds of thousands of dollars worth of annual SaaS services with internal AI prototypes within weeks. Software companies (such as ServiceNow), in order to maintain profits, will have to lay off 15% of their staff and invest the savings in more powerful AI tools to combat competition. This is a negative feedback loop without any physical braking mechanism: AI becomes stronger → companies lay off employees → money saved from layoffs is used to buy more AI computing power → AI becomes even stronger → further layoffs. The optimized white-collar workers lose income, their consumption declines, leading to a decrease in corporate revenue. To maintain profit margins, companies can only aggressively introduce AI and lay off employees. Wealth is concentrating at an unprecedented rate in the hands of a very small number of people who control computing power. In 2027, the flames of crisis will spread from the software industry to the entire "intermediary layer." Over the past fifty years, human society has built an enormous commercial empire that "monetizes friction." Because humans lack time, patience, and information asymmetry, we are willing to tolerate the commissions charged by travel platforms, insurance renewals, and real estate agents. But in the world of 2028, consumers will be fully connected to personal AI agents. These agents will tirelessly compare prices across the internet 24/7, automatically unsubscribe from forgotten SaaS subscriptions, and instantly complete due diligence and contract reviews for real estate transactions. The traditional subscription economy (betting you forget to cancel) and intermediary economy (betting you're too lazy to compare prices) will collapse overnight. Humanity's so-called "commercial stickiness," in the face of the cold, calculating power of machines, will prove to be nothing more than a warm, gentle veneer of "friction." The Remaining 24 Months For centuries, faced with Luddite panic, economists have always reassured the public with the golden rule: "Technology eliminates old jobs while creating more new ones." ATMs eliminated some tellers, but banks opened more branches; the internet killed off Yellow Pages, but created e-commerce and food delivery. But this time is different. Because in the past, all new jobs had to be done by humans. When AI evolves into a "General Intelligence" agent, it will not only be capable of performing old jobs, but its learning speed and execution cost for new jobs will also far surpass that of humans. AI does create new jobs (such as prompt word engineers and AI security reviewers), but for every new job created, dozens of traditional high-paying white-collar jobs become redundant. Moreover, these new jobs have extremely short lifespans, quickly being replaced by the next generation of stronger and cheaper agents. All the clues point to the same ending. AI will not eliminate humanity in the physical world like the Terminator, but it is reconstructing the labor value network of human society in an extremely efficient and highly rational way. But this is only the first step in the problem. By 2028, the real question is: in a society where machines create 99% of the value, but they don't consume, buy houses, see doctors, or pay taxes, how will this social cycle continue? We can laugh at Citrini's 2028 scenario as alarmist, but data from the past three years has proven that the acceleration of technology far exceeds the adaptation speed of human society. In 2023, OpenAI said it would take ten years; in 2026, Cognizant said it has already happened. So, will 2028 truly see a period of soaring GDP figures but withered consumption? Perhaps the answer lies not in technology itself, but in a more ancient question: When the primary driver of productivity is no longer humans, by what right do humans distribute wealth? Adam Smith didn't answer this question, nor did Marx. Because in their time, labor was always human. The 4,000 layoffs at Block and the 20% surge on Wall Street have already shown us which path capital has chosen. The question is, what do we choose? We must answer this question in 2026. Because we may only have 24 months left.
